Loss of arginine due to transpeptidation/Deletion of R

amidination of lysines or N-terminal amines with methyl acetimidate

project.title: Phosphoproteomic Analysis Reveals Molecular Signatures of Myocardial Infarction and Transverse Aortic Constriction in Aged Mouse Models
project.projectDescription: Project description: In this study, we performed proteome analyses to identify the key molecules that participate in aged mouse models of MI and transverse aortic constriction (TAC), the latter of which mimics increased left ventricular afterload that in the clinical setting predominantly occurs owing to aortic stenosis or systemic hypertension. B11 represents the tissues of an aged MI mouse. B17 represents the tissues of an aged TAC mouse. B15 represents the tissues of an aged Sham mouse.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: At 4 weeks after MI or TAC, heart tissues were collected and lysed in RIPA buffer with protease inhibitors and PMSF. Samples were sonicated with a Bioruptor sonicator at high intensity with 15 seconds pulses and intervals for 5 minutes and centrifuged at 14,000 g for 15 minutes. Proteins were quantified by BCA assay. Pre-chilled acetone was added to 100 μg of alkylated proteins. The extracted proteins were then solubilized in ammonium bicarbonate (100 mmol/L) with sodium deoxycholate (1%). After reducing the proteins with tris 2-carboxyethyl phosphine hydrochloride (5 mmol/L) at 55°C for 10 minutes, they were alkylated with iodoacetamide (10 mmol/L) at room temperature for 15 minutes in the dark. Subsequently, the proteins were digested overnight at 37°C using modified trypsin. The resulting tryptic peptides were desalted using a C18 cartridge and dried in a vacuum concentrator after cleaning up sodium deoxycholate with trifluoroacetic acid (2%). Next, the extracted peptides were separated using a reversed-phase column (100 μm × 150 mm) with a flow rate of 300 nL/min. For LC-MS/MS analysis, the samples were reconstituted to /uL of 0.1% FA and 2% ACN. Phosphopeptides were enriched from the lysates by using a TiO2 magnetic kit. Peptides were resuspended, desalted, and analyzed with an Orbitrap Velos mass spectrometer. The separation was performed using an EASY-nLC nano-UPLC system with a 120-minute gradient elution. The elution gradient involved changing the mobile phase composition (A: 0.1% formic acid with 2% acetonitrile, B: 80% acetonitrile with 0.1% formic acid) according to specific time intervals. The separated peptides were analyzed using a Q-Extractive mass spectrometer coupled with MS/MS analysis. The mass spectrometer employed an Orbitrap analyzer with specific resolutions for MS1 and MS2. The top 20 highest-intensity ions were subjected to fragmentation using higher-energy collisional dissociation (HCD) with a normalized collision energy of 27% and an isolation window of 2 m/z. The mass and subsequent MS/MS analyses were performed, and the instrument settings included automatic gain control targets and maximum ion injection times. Lastly, a dynamic exclusion period of 30 seconds was set to avoid repeated analysis of the same ions.
project.dataProcessingProtocol: We used MaxQuant software (version 1.5.6.0) to process the raw data. The specific parameters used included fixed modification of carbamidomethyl [C], variable modifications of oxidation [M] and acetyl [protein N‐term], allowance of three missed cleavages, and a false discovery rate (FDR) threshold of less than 0.01. Only unique and razor peptides were used for quantification. Proteins were considered successfully identified if they had a probability of greater than 99% with at least two correctly assigned peptides. Protein abundance was calculated based on normalized spectral protein intensity (LFQ intensity). Statistical analysis comparing the Log2LFQ intensity of proteins between the MI/TAC and sham groups was performed using the Perseus program, generating scatter plots and employing an unpaired student's t-test. A P value of less than .05 was considered significant. To analyze intracellular pathways, we utilized the clusterProfiler package in R to search the Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) database.

Characteristics[CleavageAgent]: trypsin digestion (enzyme-to-substrate ratio of 1:50 at 37°C for 18–20 h)
Comment[FractionationMethod]: homemade Durashell reverse-phase column (2 mg packing [3 μm, 150 Å, Agela] and two C18 layers [12 μm, Empore] in a 200-μl tip)
Comment[Instrument]: EASY-nLC 1200 nano-UPLC system coupled to a Q Exactive mass spectrometer (Thermo Scientific) with a total analysis time of 240 min per sample.
Comment[Separation]: analyzed online with a Q Exactive mass spectrometer (Thermo Scientific)
